1:
"Ye are the children of the LORD your God: ye shall not cut yourselves, nor make any baldness between your eyes for the dead." The witnesses note Israel called the children of the LORD, forbidden the heathen rites of cutting and baldness in mourning. The witnesses note that Gill notes some were children by special grace of adoption, all by national adoption — a peculiar privilege laying them under obligation to honour and obey God as their Father. The witnesses note that they were not to conform to the Gentiles' extravagant mourning for the dead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the children of the LORD — their dignity as God's sons; the cut not yourselves — no heathen self-disfigurement; the nor baldness for the dead — no hopeless mourning-rites; the sorrow with hope — the sons of God grieving differently (1 Thess. 4:13).

The witnesses agree the verse roots holiness in sonship: because they were "the children of the LORD," Israel must not disfigure themselves with the wild mourning-rites of the heathen, which sprang from a hopeless grief; the dignity of their relation as God's children forbade the customs of those without God and without hope, teaching that the sons of God are to bear even their sorrows differently from the world, sorrowing "not as others which have no hope" (1 Thess. 4:13), their very mourning marked by the restraint of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse children of the LORD: The witnesses note the the children of the LORD: their dignity as God's sons. The witnesses note the cut not yourselves: no heathen self-disfigurement. The witnesses note the nor baldness for the dead: no hopeless mourning-rites. The witnesses note the sorrow with hope: the sons of God grieving differently (1 Thess. 4:13). The expositors observe the the children of the LORD: their dignity as God's sons. The expositors observe the cut not yourselves: no heathen self-disfigurement. The expositors observe the nor baldness for the dead: no hopeless mourning-rites. The expositors observe the sorrow with hope: the sons of God grieving differently (1 Thess. 4:13). The believer is taught to mark the the children of the LORD, the cut not yourselves, the nor baldness for the dead, the sorrow with hope, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that as the children of the LORD they were not to cut themselves for the dead — holiness rooted in sonship, the sons of God sorrowing not as those without hope (1 Thess. 4:13).
2:
"For thou art an holy people unto the LORD thy God, and the LORD hath chosen thee to be a peculiar people unto himself, above all the nations that are upon the earth." The witnesses note Israel a holy and chosen people, God's peculiar treasure above all nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were set apart from all other people and devoted to God's worship, many sanctified in a special spiritual sense, and so should not conform to the customs of the Gentiles. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them a peculiar people unto God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an holy people — set apart to God; the the LORD hath chosen thee — his election of grace; the a peculiar people — his treasure above all nations; the distinct from the world — the church's calling too (1 Pet. 2:9).

The witnesses agree the ground of all these laws is Israel's election and holiness: chosen to be God's "peculiar people" above all nations, they were set apart to his worship and must keep clear of heathen ways; the holiness here is that of separation — a people consecrated to God — and it carries the same calling into the church, "a peculiar people, that ye should shew forth the praises of him" (1 Pet. 2:9), bound by their election to a life distinct from the world.

So the witnesses make the verse a holy peculiar people: The witnesses note the an holy people: set apart to God. The witnesses note the the LORD hath chosen thee: his election of grace. The witnesses note the a peculiar people: his treasure above all nations. The witnesses note the distinct from the world: the church's calling too (1 Pet. 2:9). The expositors observe the an holy people: set apart to God. The expositors observe the the LORD hath chosen thee: his election of grace. The expositors observe the a peculiar people: his treasure above all nations. The expositors observe the distinct from the world: the church's calling too (1 Pet. 2:9). The believer is taught to mark the an holy people, the the LORD hath chosen thee, the a peculiar people, the distinct from the world, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that Israel was a holy, chosen, peculiar people above all nations — their election the ground of their separation, the same calling in the church to a life distinct from the world (1 Pet. 2:9).
3:
"Thou shalt not eat any abominable thing." The witnesses note the general rule of the food laws — to eat nothing God has pronounced abominable. The witnesses note that Gill notes the abominable is so either in its own nature or because forbidden by the Lord, as declared in the following verses. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the distinctions between creatures were alike established and removed by the Creator. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat no abominable thing — the general rule; the by God's word — the distinction his appointment; the established and removed — by the same Creator (Acts 10:15); the a discipline of holiness — teaching to shun the unclean.

The witnesses agree the rule rests on the will of the Creator: what was "abominable" was so because God had pronounced it such, the clean and unclean distinguished by his word; Ellicott's note that the Creator both made and afterward removed these distinctions points forward to the gospel, where God declared all his creatures clean (Acts 10:15) — the food laws a temporary discipline of holiness and separation for Israel, teaching, under outward signs, the inward duty of shunning what is unclean and cleaving to what is pure.

So the witnesses make the verse no abominable thing: The witnesses note the eat no abominable thing: the general rule. The witnesses note the by God's word: the distinction his appointment. The witnesses note the established and removed: by the same Creator (Acts 10:15). The witnesses note the a discipline of holiness: teaching to shun the unclean. The expositors observe the eat no abominable thing: the general rule. The expositors observe the by God's word: the distinction his appointment. The expositors observe the established and removed: by the same Creator (Acts 10:15). The expositors observe the a discipline of holiness: teaching to shun the unclean. The believer is taught to mark the eat no abominable thing, the by God's word, the established and removed, the a discipline of holiness, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that Israel was to eat no abominable thing — the distinction resting on God's word, a discipline of holiness for a season, afterward removed when God declared all clean (Acts 10:15).
4:
"These are the beasts which ye shall eat: the ox, the sheep, and the goat," The witnesses note the clean beasts permitted for food — the ox, sheep, and goat, and the like. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were the beasts they might lawfully eat, allowed for their food, though not obliged to eat if they chose not. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch add the hart, roebuck, and the rest of the clean kinds. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the ox, sheep, goat — the clean beasts allowed; the lawful food — a liberty granted; the the clean wild kinds — hart, roebuck, and more; the liberty within limits — gifts enjoyed within his command.

The witnesses agree the naming of the clean beasts marks the bounds of lawful food: the ox, sheep, and goat, with the clean wild kinds, were permitted, a liberty within limits; the listing shows that God both gave his people richly to enjoy and yet set bounds upon their appetite, the clean creatures freely allowed while the unclean were forbidden — a discipline that taught Israel to receive God's gifts with thanksgiving yet within the bounds of his command, every meal a small lesson in obedience and holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse the clean beasts: The witnesses note the the ox, sheep, goat: the clean beasts allowed. The witnesses note the lawful food: a liberty granted. The witnesses note the the clean wild kinds: hart, roebuck, and more. The witnesses note the liberty within limits: gifts enjoyed within his command. The expositors observe the the ox, sheep, goat: the clean beasts allowed. The expositors observe the lawful food: a liberty granted. The expositors observe the the clean wild kinds: hart, roebuck, and more. The expositors observe the liberty within limits: gifts enjoyed within his command. The believer is taught to mark the the ox, sheep, goat, the lawful food, the the clean wild kinds, the liberty within limits, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that the ox, sheep, and goat were among the clean beasts allowed — a liberty within limits, God's gifts enjoyed within the bounds of his command, every meal a lesson in obedience.
5:
"The hart, and the roebuck, and the fallow deer, and the wild goat, and the pygarg, and the wild ox, and the chamois." The witnesses note the clean wild animals also permitted — the hart, roebuck, and other game. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were of the deer kind, agreeable food; harts were common in Canaan, and the roebuck and fallow deer were accounted delicious. The witnesses note that the various clean game were lawful to eat. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the hart and roebuck — the clean deer kinds; the fallow deer, wild goat — accounted delicate food; the the wild ox and chamois — the game allowed; the bounty within bounds — richly provided, freely fed.

The witnesses agree the list of clean game shows the breadth of the liberty God allowed: not only the domestic beasts but the wild deer and their kinds, accounted delicate food, were given for Israel's enjoyment; the abundance of permitted creatures over against the few forbidden teaches that God's restrictions were no grudging of good things but a gracious bounty within bounds, his people richly provided and freely fed, the clean creatures of field and forest theirs to enjoy with thanksgiving.

So the witnesses make the verse the clean game: The witnesses note the the hart and roebuck: the clean deer kinds. The witnesses note the fallow deer, wild goat: accounted delicate food. The witnesses note the the wild ox and chamois: the game allowed. The witnesses note the bounty within bounds: richly provided, freely fed. The expositors observe the the hart and roebuck: the clean deer kinds. The expositors observe the fallow deer, wild goat: accounted delicate food. The expositors observe the the wild ox and chamois: the game allowed. The expositors observe the bounty within bounds: richly provided, freely fed. The believer is taught to mark the the hart and roebuck, the fallow deer, wild goat, the the wild ox and chamois, the bounty within bounds, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that the hart, roebuck, and other clean game were permitted — the breadth of God's liberty, his bounty within bounds, his people richly provided to enjoy with thanksgiving.
6:
"And every beast that parteth the hoof, and cleaveth the cleft into two claws, and cheweth the cud among the beasts, that ye shall eat." The witnesses note the two marks of a clean beast — the parted hoof and the chewing of the cud. The witnesses note that Gill notes two general rules: a beast that both parted the hoof and chewed the cud might be eaten, but one that did only the one, as the camel, hare, or coney, might not. The witnesses note that both marks together made the beast clean. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the parteth the hoof — the divided foot; the cheweth the cud — the inward digesting; the both together — the clean beast known; the a parable of holiness — right walking and meditation on the word.

The witnesses agree the double test — divided hoof and chewed cud — gave a plain rule for discerning the clean; the requiring of both marks together has long been read by the fathers as a parable of holiness: the divided hoof a figure of right walking and discernment, the chewing of the cud a figure of meditation on God's word, so that the truly clean soul both walks uprightly and inwardly digests the truth; the bodily rule taught a spiritual lesson, that the clean before God are marked by both a holy life and a meditative heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the two marks: The witnesses note the parteth the hoof: the divided foot. The witnesses note the cheweth the cud: the inward digesting. The witnesses note the both together: the clean beast known. The witnesses note the a parable of holiness: right walking and meditation on the word. The expositors observe the parteth the hoof: the divided foot. The expositors observe the cheweth the cud: the inward digesting. The expositors observe the both together: the clean beast known. The expositors observe the a parable of holiness: right walking and meditation on the word. The believer is taught to mark the parteth the hoof, the cheweth the cud, the both together, the a parable of holiness, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that the clean beast both parted the hoof and chewed the cud — a plain rule, long read as a parable of holiness, the clean soul marked by both an upright walk and meditation on God's word.
9:
"These ye shall eat of all that are in the waters: all that have fins and scales shall ye eat:" The witnesses note the mark of clean water creatures — fins and scales. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fishes that have fins and scales might be eaten, but those that have not were not to be eaten. The witnesses note that this was the rule for all that are in the waters. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all in the waters — the rule for fish; the fins and scales — the mark of the clean; the those without — not to be eaten; the a figure of holiness — resisting the stream, guarded by righteousness.

The witnesses agree the rule for the waters, like that for the beasts, set a clear and simple mark: only the fish with fins and scales were clean; the fins, by which the fish moves against the current, and the scales, which guard it, have been taken by the fathers as figures of the soul that makes way against the world's stream and is guarded by the armour of righteousness — so that here too the bodily distinction shadowed a spiritual one, the clean marked by the power to resist and the covering to protect, a small parable of the holy life set in the law of food.

So the witnesses make the verse fins and scales: The witnesses note the all in the waters: the rule for fish. The witnesses note the fins and scales: the mark of the clean. The witnesses note the those without: not to be eaten. The witnesses note the a figure of holiness: resisting the stream, guarded by righteousness. The expositors observe the all in the waters: the rule for fish. The expositors observe the fins and scales: the mark of the clean. The expositors observe the those without: not to be eaten. The expositors observe the a figure of holiness: resisting the stream, guarded by righteousness. The believer is taught to mark the all in the waters, the fins and scales, the those without, the a figure of holiness, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that the clean fish had fins and scales — a clear mark, taken by the fathers as a figure of the soul that resists the world's stream and is guarded by righteousness, the law of food shadowing the holy life.
11:
"Of all clean birds ye shall eat." The witnesses note the general permission of all clean birds, the unclean named by exception. The witnesses note that Gill notes the clean birds, by the Targum, are those with a craw, a naked crop, a superfluous talon, and not rapacious; only the unclean are named by exception, so all others might be reckoned clean. The witnesses note that Maimonides observes only the unclean are numbered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all clean birds — freely allowed; the the unclean named — only the few exceptions; the the clean too many to list — the bounty wide; the gifts outrun prohibitions — god giving richly, forbidding sparingly.

The witnesses agree the manner of the law — naming the few unclean and allowing all the rest — shows the largeness of God's liberality: the clean birds were too many to list, so only the forbidden were named, leaving the great number free; the very form of the command teaches that God's gifts far outrun his prohibitions, his bounty wide and his restraints few, the people left a generous liberty in all but the named exceptions — a pattern of a God who gives richly and forbids sparingly.

So the witnesses make the verse all clean birds: The witnesses note the all clean birds: freely allowed. The witnesses note the the unclean named: only the few exceptions. The witnesses note the the clean too many to list: the bounty wide. The witnesses note the gifts outrun prohibitions: God giving richly, forbidding sparingly. The expositors observe the all clean birds: freely allowed. The expositors observe the the unclean named: only the few exceptions. The expositors observe the the clean too many to list: the bounty wide. The expositors observe the gifts outrun prohibitions: God giving richly, forbidding sparingly. The believer is taught to mark the all clean birds, the the unclean named, the the clean too many to list, the gifts outrun prohibitions, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that all clean birds were allowed, only the unclean named — the largeness of God's liberality, his gifts far outrunning his prohibitions, a generous liberty in all but the few exceptions.
12:
"But these are they of which ye shall not eat: the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray," The witnesses note the unclean birds begin to be named, the first being the eagle and other birds of prey. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jarchi observes the unclean birds are particularly mentioned to teach that the clean are more than the unclean, so the fewer are named; these are the same names as in Leviticus, except one, the glede. The witnesses note that the unclean were forbidden by name. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the these ye shall not eat — the forbidden named; the the eagle, ospray — birds of prey; the the fewer named — the clean being more; the a figure of abhorrence — shunning rapine and corruption.

The witnesses agree the naming of the unclean birds, beginning with the eagle and the birds of prey, marks a class set apart as forbidden: the rapacious and the carrion-eaters were unclean, a fitting figure, for the fathers have noted that the forbidden birds are largely those that prey on others or feed on death; the law thus taught, under the form of food, an abhorrence of cruelty, rapine, and corruption — the clean soul to be no bird of prey, but gentle and pure, shunning what feeds on violence and decay.

So the witnesses make the verse the unclean birds: The witnesses note the these ye shall not eat: the forbidden named. The witnesses note the the eagle, ospray: birds of prey. The witnesses note the the fewer named: the clean being more. The witnesses note the a figure of abhorrence: shunning rapine and corruption. The expositors observe the these ye shall not eat: the forbidden named. The expositors observe the the eagle, ospray: birds of prey. The expositors observe the the fewer named: the clean being more. The expositors observe the a figure of abhorrence: shunning rapine and corruption. The believer is taught to mark the these ye shall not eat, the the eagle, ospray, the the fewer named, the a figure of abhorrence, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that the unclean birds, beginning with the eagle and the birds of prey, were forbidden — the law teaching, under the form of food, an abhorrence of cruelty, rapine, and corruption.
13:
"And the glede, and the kite, and the vulture after his kind," The witnesses note further unclean birds — the glede, kite, and vulture, with some variation in the names. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the word daah is improperly translated vulture in Leviticus, meaning rather a kite or glede, while daiyah here means a different bird, properly the vulture. The witnesses note that Ellicott compares the list with Leviticus 11. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the glede, kite, vulture — further unclean birds; the after his kind — each sort reckoned; the the names varied — noted by the learned, the sense the same; the exactness of the law — every kind precisely marked.

The witnesses agree the careful naming, with its slight differences from the Leviticus list, shows the exactness of the law in marking the unclean: the glede, kite, and vulture — again birds of prey and carrion — are particularly named, the variation in the words noted by the learned but the sense the same; the precision teaches that God's distinctions are exact and not careless, every kind reckoned, and the same lesson pressed, that the people of God are to keep clear of all that the vulture and kite represent, the unclean appetite for prey and death.

So the witnesses make the verse the glede and kite: The witnesses note the the glede, kite, vulture: further unclean birds. The witnesses note the after his kind: each sort reckoned. The witnesses note the the names varied: noted by the learned, the sense the same. The witnesses note the exactness of the law: every kind precisely marked. The expositors observe the the glede, kite, vulture: further unclean birds. The expositors observe the after his kind: each sort reckoned. The expositors observe the the names varied: noted by the learned, the sense the same. The expositors observe the exactness of the law: every kind precisely marked. The believer is taught to mark the the glede, kite, vulture, the after his kind, the the names varied, the exactness of the law, and to be holy, as a child of God. The verse thus records that the glede, kite, and vulture are named among the unclean — the exactness of the law marking every kind, pressing again the abhorrence of the unclean appetite for prey and death.
19:
"And every creeping thing that flieth is unclean unto you: they shall not be eaten." The witnesses note the flying creeping things — insects and the like — reckoned unclean and forbidden. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Targum of Jonathan reckons all flies, wasps or hornets, and worms that fly as birds among the unclean. The witnesses note that these were not to be eaten. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the every creeping thing that flieth — the flying insects; the is unclean — forbidden as food; the they shall not be eaten — the least things ordered; the holiness in small matters — careful obedience even in the least.

The witnesses agree the law extends the rule of cleanness even to the least creatures: the flying insects and creeping things were unclean, forbidden as food; the reaching of the law to such small matters teaches the thoroughness of the holiness God required, that nothing was too small to fall under his ordering, and that his people were to be discerning in the least things as in the great — a discipline that schooled them to a careful and entire obedience, holy even in the smallest details of daily life.

So the witnesses make the verse flying creeping things: The witnesses note the every creeping thing that flieth: the flying insects. The witnesses note the is unclean: forbidden as food. The witnesses note the they shall not be eaten: the least things ordered. The witnesses note the holiness in small matters: careful obedience even in the least. The expositors observe the every creeping thing that flieth: the flying insects. The expositors observe the is unclean: forbidden as food. The expositors observe the they shall not be eaten: the least things ordered. The expositors observe the holiness in small matters: careful obedience even in the least. The believer is taught to mark the every creeping thing that flieth, the is unclean, the they shall not be eaten, the holiness in small matters, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that the flying creeping things were unclean — the law reaching even to the least creatures, schooling Israel to a careful holiness in the smallest details of daily life.
20:
"But of all clean fowls ye may eat." The witnesses note the permission, after the unclean were named, to eat all clean fowls. The witnesses note that Gill notes that of all fowls but those excepted they might eat; Aben Ezra instances the clean locust as a fowl that might be eaten, and the Targum allows every clean locust. The witnesses note that all clean fowls were lawful food. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all clean fowls — freely allowed; the but those excepted — the unclean few; the the clean locust — among the permitted; the liberty within the law — bounty exceeding the bans.

The witnesses agree the closing permission again shows the breadth of the liberty within the law: once the unclean were named, "all clean fowls" remained free to eat, the forbidden few and the allowed many; the law of food, for all its restrictions, left God's people a wide and thankful liberty, the great bulk of the creatures clean and given for their use — a reminder that God's commands, even in their limits, breathe more of bounty than of bondage, his gifts far exceeding his bans.

So the witnesses make the verse all clean fowls: The witnesses note the all clean fowls: freely allowed. The witnesses note the but those excepted: the unclean few. The witnesses note the the clean locust: among the permitted. The witnesses note the liberty within the law: bounty exceeding the bans. The expositors observe the all clean fowls: freely allowed. The expositors observe the but those excepted: the unclean few. The expositors observe the the clean locust: among the permitted. The expositors observe the liberty within the law: bounty exceeding the bans. The believer is taught to mark the all clean fowls, the but those excepted, the the clean locust, the liberty within the law, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that all clean fowls might be eaten — the breadth of liberty within the law, the forbidden few and the allowed many, God's commands breathing more of bounty than of bondage.
21:
"Ye shall not eat of any thing that dieth of itself... for thou art an holy people unto the LORD thy God. Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk." The witnesses note the ban on eating what dies of itself, and the strange prohibition against seething a kid in its mother's milk. The witnesses note that Gill notes the law against eating what dies of itself is repeated from Leviticus. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Calmet thinks the seething of a kid in its mother's milk refers to the paschal lamb, while others connect it with heathen custom; Ellicott treats it likewise. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat not what dies of itself — no carrion for the holy; the for thou art holy — the ground of the ban; the seethe not a kid in its mother's milk — against rite or cruelty; the reverence and tenderness — holiness reaching into the home.

The witnesses agree these closing food-laws are grounded again in holiness: the people were not to eat carrion, "for thou art an holy people," and not to seethe a kid in its mother's milk — a prohibition variously explained as guarding against a heathen rite or against an unnatural cruelty; whatever its precise ground, the law breathes the same spirit, that God's holy people were to keep clear of what is unclean or unkind, even their cooking marked by reverence and tenderness, holiness reaching into the common usages of the home.

So the witnesses make the verse holiness in food: The witnesses note the eat not what dies of itself: no carrion for the holy. The witnesses note the for thou art holy: the ground of the ban. The witnesses note the seethe not a kid in its mother's milk: against rite or cruelty. The witnesses note the reverence and tenderness: holiness reaching into the home. The expositors observe the eat not what dies of itself: no carrion for the holy. The expositors observe the for thou art holy: the ground of the ban. The expositors observe the seethe not a kid in its mother's milk: against rite or cruelty. The expositors observe the reverence and tenderness: holiness reaching into the home. The believer is taught to mark the eat not what dies of itself, the for thou art holy, the seethe not a kid in its mother's milk, the reverence and tenderness, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that they were not to eat carrion nor seethe a kid in its mother's milk — the food-laws grounded in holiness, God's people keeping clear of the unclean and the unkind, reverence reaching into the common usages of the home.
22:
"Thou shalt truly tithe all the increase of thy seed, that the field bringeth forth year by year." The witnesses note the command to tithe faithfully all the yearly increase of the field — the festival tithe. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was a different tithe from that given to the Levites; this the owners themselves ate of, the tithing left to be made faithfully by them. The witnesses note that it was to be of all the increase, year by year. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the truly tithe — an exact, honest rendering; the all the increase — of seed and field; the year by year — a yearly devotion; the all from God — the harvest his gift confessed (Prov. 3:9).

The witnesses agree the festival tithe was a yearly act of faithful devotion: distinct from the Levites' tithe, this portion the owner himself set apart and brought to eat before the LORD, the tithing entrusted to his own honesty "year by year"; the command to "truly tithe" presses the duty of an exact and faithful rendering to God of a part of all his increase, the acknowledgment that the whole harvest is his gift, and the setting-apart of a portion a yearly confession that all we have comes from him (cf. Prov. 3:9).

So the witnesses make the verse the faithful tithe: The witnesses note the truly tithe: an exact, honest rendering. The witnesses note the all the increase: of seed and field. The witnesses note the year by year: a yearly devotion. The witnesses note the all from God: the harvest his gift confessed (Prov. 3:9). The expositors observe the truly tithe: an exact, honest rendering. The expositors observe the all the increase: of seed and field. The expositors observe the year by year: a yearly devotion. The expositors observe the all from God: the harvest his gift confessed (Prov. 3:9). The believer is taught to mark the truly tithe, the all the increase, the year by year, the all from God, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that Israel was to truly tithe all the yearly increase — a faithful rendering to God of a part of all his gift, the yearly confession that the whole harvest comes from him (Prov. 3:9).
23:
"And thou shalt eat before the LORD thy God, in the place which he shall choose to place his name there... that thou mayest learn to fear the LORD thy God always." The witnesses note the tithe eaten before the LORD at the chosen place, that they might learn to fear him. The witnesses note that Gill notes this second tithe was eaten by the owners before the Lord at the chosen place. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the end — that thou mayest learn to fear the LORD thy God always. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat before the LORD — at the place of his name; the the festival tithe — a sacred yearly meal; the that thou mayest learn — the end of it; the to fear the LORD always — the table a school of reverence.

The witnesses agree the festival tithe had a gracious spiritual end: it was to be eaten "before the LORD" at the place of his name, that by this yearly, sacred meal in his presence the people "might learn to fear" him; the joining of feasting and reverence shows that God meant his worship to be both glad and holy, the table before him a school of the fear of God, where joy in his gifts and awe of his presence met — religion not only of duty but of delight, the heart taught reverence by communion with God over his bounty.

So the witnesses make the verse learn to fear the LORD: The witnesses note the eat before the LORD: at the place of his name. The witnesses note the the festival tithe: a sacred yearly meal. The witnesses note the that thou mayest learn: the end of it. The witnesses note the to fear the LORD always: the table a school of reverence. The expositors observe the eat before the LORD: at the place of his name. The expositors observe the the festival tithe: a sacred yearly meal. The expositors observe the that thou mayest learn: the end of it. The expositors observe the to fear the LORD always: the table a school of reverence. The believer is taught to mark the eat before the LORD, the the festival tithe, the that thou mayest learn, the to fear the LORD always, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that the tithe was eaten before the LORD that they might learn to fear him — the sacred meal a school of reverence, joy in his gifts and awe of his presence meeting at his table.
24:
"And if the way be too long for thee, so that thou art not able to carry it... when the LORD thy God hath blessed thee:" The witnesses note the provision for those who lived too far to carry their tithe in kind to the chosen place. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was for the Israelite whose dwelling was far from the place. The witnesses note that Calvin, weighing whether this refers to the tithes or the firstfruits, notes the redemption-rule with its added fifth (Lev. 27:31) is here omitted. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if the way be too long — too far to carry in kind; the when God hath blessed thee — the increase great; the another way provided — the duty kept, the manner eased; the God considers our weakness — the form made bearable.

The witnesses agree the law made gracious provision for distance: the Israelite who lived too far to carry his tithe in kind was not thereby excused, but given another way to render it; the tender accommodation shows that God's commands are not rigid burdens but fitted to his people's circumstances, the duty kept while the manner is eased — a reminder that God considers our weakness and distance, requiring the substance of obedience while making its outward form bearable, that none be shut out from worship by hardship.

So the witnesses make the verse provision for distance: The witnesses note the if the way be too long: too far to carry in kind. The witnesses note the when God hath blessed thee: the increase great. The witnesses note the another way provided: the duty kept, the manner eased. The witnesses note the God considers our weakness: the form made bearable. The expositors observe the if the way be too long: too far to carry in kind. The expositors observe the when God hath blessed thee: the increase great. The expositors observe the another way provided: the duty kept, the manner eased. The expositors observe the God considers our weakness: the form made bearable. The believer is taught to mark the if the way be too long, the when God hath blessed thee, the another way provided, the God considers our weakness, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that provision was made for those too far to carry the tithe in kind — God's command fitted to his people's circumstances, the substance of obedience kept while its form was eased, that none be shut out by hardship.
25:
"Then shalt thou turn it into money, and bind up the money in thine hand, and shalt go unto the place which the LORD thy God shall choose:" The witnesses note the remedy — to turn the tithe into money, carry it, and go to the chosen place. The witnesses note that Gill notes the tithe was turned into money, which would be lighter and easier of carriage. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read "give it up for silver," binding the silver in the hand and going to the place. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turn it into money — lighter for carriage; the bind up the money — the silver in hand; the go to the chosen place — the worship kept whole; the service made practicable — devotion not hindered by difficulty.

The witnesses agree the turning of the tithe into money was a practical and gracious ease: instead of dragging produce a long way, the Israelite might sell it, bind the silver, and carry that to the chosen place — the worship kept whole while the burden was lightened; the arrangement shows God's wisdom in making his service practicable, that the heart's devotion need not be hindered by the body's difficulty, the substance of the offering preserved though its form was changed for convenience, true worship not bound to one cumbersome manner.

So the witnesses make the verse turn it into money: The witnesses note the turn it into money: lighter for carriage. The witnesses note the bind up the money: the silver in hand. The witnesses note the go to the chosen place: the worship kept whole. The witnesses note the service made practicable: devotion not hindered by difficulty. The expositors observe the turn it into money: lighter for carriage. The expositors observe the bind up the money: the silver in hand. The expositors observe the go to the chosen place: the worship kept whole. The expositors observe the service made practicable: devotion not hindered by difficulty. The believer is taught to mark the turn it into money, the bind up the money, the go to the chosen place, the service made practicable, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that the tithe might be turned into money and carried to the chosen place — the worship kept whole while the burden was lightened, God making his service practicable, true devotion not bound to one cumbersome form.
26:
"And thou shalt bestow that money for whatsoever thy soul lusteth after... and thou shalt eat there before the LORD thy God, and thou shalt rejoice, thou, and thine household," The witnesses note the freedom to buy whatever the heart desired, and to eat and rejoice before the LORD with the household. The witnesses note that Gill notes he might buy what provision he would, what he best liked and was most agreeable to his appetite. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the buying of oxen, sheep, wine, or strong drink, whatsoever the soul desired. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the whatsoever thy soul desireth — free to buy as he liked; the eat before the LORD — a feast in his presence; the rejoice with thy household — holy gladness; the religion sanctifies joy — rejoice in the Lord always (Phil. 4:4).

The witnesses agree the festival tithe was meant for holy joy: the Israelite might buy with the money whatever his soul desired — oxen, sheep, wine — and eat it before the LORD, rejoicing with his household; the law thus sanctified gladness, making a feast of thanksgiving in God's presence a part of worship, that the people delight in his gifts before him; true religion is shown here to be no enemy of joy but its sanctifier, the table of plenty spread before the LORD a place of grateful rejoicing (cf. Phil. 4:4, rejoice in the Lord always).

So the witnesses make the verse eat and rejoice: The witnesses note the whatsoever thy soul desireth: free to buy as he liked. The witnesses note the eat before the LORD: a feast in his presence. The witnesses note the rejoice with thy household: holy gladness. The witnesses note the religion sanctifies joy: rejoice in the Lord always (Phil. 4:4). The expositors observe the whatsoever thy soul desireth: free to buy as he liked. The expositors observe the eat before the LORD: a feast in his presence. The expositors observe the rejoice with thy household: holy gladness. The expositors observe the religion sanctifies joy: rejoice in the Lord always (Phil. 4:4). The believer is taught to mark the whatsoever thy soul desireth, the eat before the LORD, the rejoice with thy household, the religion sanctifies joy, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that he might buy what he desired and rejoice before the LORD with his household — the festival tithe sanctifying gladness, true religion no enemy of joy but its sanctifier, rejoicing in the Lord (Phil. 4:4).
27:
"And the Levite that is within thy gates; thou shalt not forsake him; for he hath no part nor inheritance with thee." The witnesses note the charge not to forget the Levite, who had no inheritance, in the festal joy. The witnesses note that Gill notes, with Jarchi, that as he was not to be forsaken in the first tithe, so neither here; he was not to be left behind but taken to share the entertainment. The witnesses note that the Levite had no part nor inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Levite in thy gates — god's servant near; the forsake him not — bring him to the feast; the no part nor inheritance — having no land of his own; the joy shared — plenty given that we may share it.

The witnesses agree the festal joy was to be shared, not selfish: the Levite, who had no land of his own, was not to be forgotten but brought in to the feast, the worshipper's gladness made a means of kindness to God's servant; the charge teaches that true rejoicing before the LORD includes remembering the needy, that our plenty is given partly that we may share it, and that the joy of the godly overflows in care for those who have no portion — religion and charity joined at the same table.

So the witnesses make the verse forsake not the Levite: The witnesses note the the Levite in thy gates: God's servant near. The witnesses note the forsake him not: bring him to the feast. The witnesses note the no part nor inheritance: having no land of his own. The witnesses note the joy shared: plenty given that we may share it. The expositors observe the the Levite in thy gates: God's servant near. The expositors observe the forsake him not: bring him to the feast. The expositors observe the no part nor inheritance: having no land of his own. The expositors observe the joy shared: plenty given that we may share it. The believer is taught to mark the the Levite in thy gates, the forsake him not, the no part nor inheritance, the joy shared, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that the Levite was not to be forgotten in the feast — the festal joy shared, not selfish, true rejoicing before the LORD including care for those who have no portion, religion and charity at one table.
28:
"At the end of three years thou shalt bring forth all the tithe of thine increase the same year, and shalt lay it up within thy gates:" The witnesses note the third-year tithe, laid up at home for the poor rather than carried to the chosen place. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, according to Aben Ezra, this was a third tithe; the Jewish writers and Tobit speak variously of it, but it agrees best with the care of the stranger, fatherless, and widow that follows. The witnesses note that it was laid up within their own gates the same year. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the at the end of three years — the special year; the all the tithe of that year — set apart wholly; the laid up within thy gates — kept at home for the needy; the worship and mercy joined — the same increase serving both.

The witnesses agree the third-year tithe turned the offering toward the poor at home: instead of being eaten before the LORD at the chosen place, this tithe was laid up "within thy gates" for the needy of the locality; the alternating use of the tithe — now for festal joy before God, now for the poor at home — shows that the worship of God and the care of the poor are bound together in his law, the same increase serving both the praise of God and the relief of man, devotion and mercy flowing from one stream.

So the witnesses make the verse the third-year tithe: The witnesses note the at the end of three years: the special year. The witnesses note the all the tithe of that year: set apart wholly. The witnesses note the laid up within thy gates: kept at home for the needy. The witnesses note the worship and mercy joined: the same increase serving both. The expositors observe the at the end of three years: the special year. The expositors observe the all the tithe of that year: set apart wholly. The expositors observe the laid up within thy gates: kept at home for the needy. The expositors observe the worship and mercy joined: the same increase serving both. The believer is taught to mark the at the end of three years, the all the tithe of that year, the laid up within thy gates, the worship and mercy joined, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that the third-year tithe was laid up at home for the poor — worship and the care of the needy bound together in God's law, the same increase serving both his praise and the relief of man.
29:
"And the Levite... and the stranger, and the fatherless, and the widow, which are within thy gates, shall come, and shall eat and be satisfied; that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all the work of thine hand." The witnesses note the poor — Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow — fed from the tithe, that God might bless the giver. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Levite, having no inheritance, was to share this poor's tithe, together with the stranger, fatherless, and widow within their gates. The witnesses note that the end was that the LORD might bless them in all the work of their hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow — all the needy gathered; the eat and be satisfied — fully fed from the tithe; the that the LORD may bless thee — the giver enriched; the mercy lent to the LORD — the open hand blessed (Prov. 19:17).

The witnesses agree the third-year tithe gathered all the needy to a common table and joined giving to blessing: the Levite, stranger, fatherless, and widow were to "eat and be satisfied," and the promise was added "that the LORD may bless thee" — the giver's liberality the way to his own enrichment; the law teaches that mercy to the poor is lent to the LORD, who repays the bountiful, and that the open hand is blessed, "he that hath pity upon the poor lendeth unto the LORD" (Prov. 19:17), charity and blessing meeting in the care of the needy.

So the witnesses make the verse fed and blessed: The witnesses note the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow: all the needy gathered. The witnesses note the eat and be satisfied: fully fed from the tithe. The witnesses note the that the LORD may bless thee: the giver enriched. The witnesses note the mercy lent to the LORD: the open hand blessed (Prov. 19:17). The expositors observe the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow: all the needy gathered. The expositors observe the eat and be satisfied: fully fed from the tithe. The expositors observe the that the LORD may bless thee: the giver enriched. The expositors observe the mercy lent to the LORD: the open hand blessed (Prov. 19:17). The believer is taught to mark the Levite, stranger, fatherless, widow, the eat and be satisfied, the that the LORD may bless thee, the mercy lent to the LORD, and to rejoice before the LORD with the poor. The verse thus records that the poor were fed from the tithe, that God might bless the giver — mercy to the needy lent to the LORD who repays the bountiful, charity and blessing meeting in the care of the poor (Prov. 19:17).
